choices.A: Cannot calculate without correlated trace data; instrument end-to-end traces first
choices.B: End-to-end p99 = 200ms (max), success rate = 99.7% (average); all SLOs are met
choices.C: End-to-end p99 = 430ms (sum), success rate = 97.1% (product); Inventory Agent is the bottleneck for both latency and errors; optimize Inventory Agent's database queries and add caching for frequent lookups, plus implement retry with backoff for transient errors to bring error rate below 0.5%
choices.D: End-to-end p99 = 108ms (average), success rate = 97.1%; Pricing Agent is the bottleneck

questions.explanation: Step 1: In a serial pipeline, p99 latency approximates the sum of individual p99s (worst case): 50+80+200+100 = 430ms. Success rate = 0.999 * 0.995 * 0.98 * 0.997 = ~0.971 (97.1%). Step 2: p99 is under 500ms (OK), but success rate is 97.1% vs 99% SLO (VIOLATION). Inventory Agent's 2% error rate is the dominant contributor. Step 3: Targeting Inventory Agent's error rate provides the most impact on the SLO violation.

questions.question: After implementing a PDCA cycle for reducing deployment failures: Plan: implement canary deployments. Do: rolled out canary to 3 services. Check: deployment failure rate dropped from 8% to 3%, but canary analysis has a 15% false-positive rate causing unnecessary rollbacks. Act: need to refine. Step 1: Evaluate the Check phase results. Step 2: Determine whether the cycle succeeded. Step 3: Design the next PDCA iteration.

choices.A: The cycle failed because of false positives; revert canary deployments entirely
choices.B: The cycle succeeded; ignore false positives since they are better than failures
choices.C: The cycle fully succeeded; deploy canary to all services as-is
choices.D: Partial success: failure rate improved (8% -> 3%) but false positives (15%) reduce deployment velocity and engineer trust. Next PDCA: Plan: tune canary analysis thresholds using historical data to reduce false positives below 5%. Do: implement tuned thresholds on the existing 3 services. Check: measure both failure rate and false positive rate over 2 weeks. Act: if both metrics meet targets, expand to remaining services

questions.explanation: Step 1: The primary metric improved (8% -> 3% failure rate) but a secondary problem emerged (15% false positive rate). Step 2: Partial success: the intervention works but needs refinement. This is exactly what PDCA is designed for. Step 3: The Act phase feeds into the next Plan: address the false positive rate specifically, with clear targets (< 5%), a scoped test (same 3 services), and a defined check period.

questions.question: A burn rate alert fires: the 30-day error budget is being consumed at 14.4x the sustainable rate over the last 5 minutes. The SLO is 99.9% (43.2 minutes/month). Step 1: Calculate how long until budget exhaustion at this rate. Step 2: Determine the appropriate response. Step 3: Define when to escalate from warning to emergency.

choices.A: At 14.4x burn rate, the 30-day budget would be exhausted in 30/14.4 = ~2.08 days, but this is a 5-minute window measurement. Appropriate response: page immediately (14.4x over 5 min is a fast-burn alert), investigate for active incident, and check if the spike is transient (deployment, traffic anomaly) or sustained. Escalate to emergency if the 1-hour burn rate also exceeds 6x (sustained fast burn), indicating systemic failure rather than a spike
choices.B: Budget exhaustion in 2 days; monitor the situation; escalate if it continues for 24 hours
choices.C: Budget exhaustion in 30 minutes; trigger automatic rollback of all recent deployments
choices.D: 14.4x burn rate is normal during traffic spikes; no action needed

questions.explanation: Step 1: At 14.4x, the 30-day budget depletes in 30/14.4 ~ 50 hours. But the 5-minute window is short; the rate may not sustain. Step 2: A 14.4x 5-minute burn rate warrants immediate investigation (page). It is not yet an emergency; it could be a transient spike. Step 3: Escalate if the 1-hour sliding window also shows elevated burn (e.g., >6x), confirming sustained consumption rather than a momentary spike.

questions.question: You need to design an alerting strategy for a new service. Current state: 50 alerts configured, team receives 200 alerts/week, 85% are non-actionable (noise). On-call engineers report alert fatigue and have started ignoring low-severity pages. Step 1: Diagnose the alerting system's health. Step 2: Propose an alerting framework redesign. Step 3: Define success metrics for the new framework.

choices.A: Diagnosis: 85% noise rate indicates alerts are triggering on symptoms (CPU, memory) rather than user-impacting signals, and thresholds are too sensitive. Redesign: restructure around symptom-based alerts on golden signals (user-facing latency, error rate), remove non-actionable alerts, add severity-appropriate routing (page for SEV-1/2, ticket for SEV-3/4), and attach runbook links to every alert. Success metrics: noise rate below 20%, alert-to-incident ratio above 50%, MTTD unchanged or improved, on-call satisfaction score improvement
choices.B: Add more alerts to cover every possible failure mode; hire more on-call staff to handle volume
choices.C: Turn off all alerts and rely on customer reports for incident detection
choices.D: Reduce all alert thresholds to trigger only on complete outages; success: fewer alerts

questions.explanation: Step 1: 85% noise rate means the alerting system is counterproductive; it teaches engineers to ignore alerts. Step 2: Shifting from cause-based to symptom-based alerting (golden signals) reduces noise while maintaining coverage. Severity-based routing ensures pages are reserved for true urgencies. Runbooks make every alert actionable. Step 3: The noise rate, alert-to-incident ratio, and MTTD together verify that quality improved without sacrificing detection speed.

questions.question: A resilience test reveals that when a payment service goes down, the order service continues accepting orders for 5 minutes, creating 2,000 orders that cannot be processed. The team debates two solutions: (A) Add synchronous validation of payment service availability before accepting orders. (B) Add a message queue between order and payment services. Step 1: Analyze the trade-offs of each approach. Step 2: Evaluate which approach better supports graceful degradation. Step 3: Recommend a solution with justification.

choices.A: Choose (A): synchronous check is simpler; reject all orders when payment is down
choices.B: Choose (B): the message queue decouples the services, allowing orders to be accepted and queued during payment outages, then processed when payment recovers. This provides graceful degradation (orders are not lost or rejected) versus approach (A) which causes total order rejection during outages. Trade-off: (B) adds complexity (queue management, idempotency, eventual consistency) but significantly improves user experience and revenue preservation during partial outages
choices.C: Choose both: implement synchronous check AND queue simultaneously for defense in depth
choices.D: Choose neither: the 5-minute gap is acceptable; accept the occasional failed orders

questions.explanation: Step 1: (A) is simple but creates an availability dependency: order service fails whenever payment fails. (B) decouples but adds queue management complexity. Step 2: Graceful degradation means maintaining core functionality with reduced capability. (B) lets orders flow during payment outages; (A) rejects them entirely. Step 3: (B) better serves users and revenue, with the complexity managed by established patterns (idempotent consumers, dead letter queues, reconciliation).

questions.question: A compliance audit requires evidence that AI model outputs are logged and auditable for the past 12 months. Your system logs inputs/outputs but: (A) PII is present in 30% of logs. (B) Log retention is set to 90 days. (C) No integrity protection (logs could be modified). Step 1: Identify each compliance gap. Step 2: Assess regulatory risk. Step 3: Design a compliant logging architecture.

choices.A: Delete all logs containing PII; retain remaining logs for 12 months
choices.B: Gaps: (A) PII in audit logs violates data minimization principles and creates privacy liability. (B) 90-day retention fails the 12-month requirement. (C) Unprotected logs cannot serve as trustworthy audit evidence. Risk: audit failure on all three counts, with PII exposure creating additional regulatory liability. Architecture: implement PII detection and tokenization in the logging pipeline, extend retention to 13 months with tiered storage (hot 30 days, cold 13 months), add append-only storage with cryptographic integrity verification (hash chains or WORM storage) to prevent tampering
choices.C: Extend retention to 12 months; the other issues are minor
choices.D: Encrypt all logs and provide decryption keys only to auditors

questions.explanation: Step 1: Three distinct gaps: PII exposure, insufficient retention, and no integrity. Step 2: Each gap is individually a compliance failure; together they represent systemic logging inadequacy. Step 3: PII tokenization preserves auditability while removing sensitive data. Extended retention with tiering manages cost. Append-only storage with integrity verification ensures logs cannot be tampered with, satisfying audit evidence requirements.

questions.question: Your growth projection shows traffic doubling in 6 months. Current system: 3 application servers (60% utilized), 1 database primary + 2 read replicas (database primary at 75% CPU, read replicas at 40%), Redis cache with 85% hit rate. Step 1: Identify which tier will bottleneck first. Step 2: Calculate when the bottleneck hits. Step 3: Design a scaling plan with timeline.

choices.A: Redis cache bottlenecks first; increase cache memory
choices.B: Database primary bottlenecks first: at 75% utilization with 2x growth, it reaches 150% (overload) while app servers reach only 120% and are horizontally scalable. Timeline: database primary hits critical (>85%) at ~1.5 months of growth. Scaling plan: Month 1: implement write sharding or connection pooling to reduce primary load. Month 2: add a read replica and increase cache hit rate to 92%+ to reduce read load on primary. Month 3: evaluate database vertical scaling or introducing a write replica set. App tier: add auto-scaling group by month 3 (less urgent since horizontal scaling is straightforward)
choices.C: All tiers scale linearly; double everything at month 6
choices.D: Application servers bottleneck first; add 3 more servers in month 5

questions.explanation: Step 1: Database primary at 75% is the highest utilized non-horizontally-scalable component. App servers can scale horizontally. Redis can scale horizontally with clustering. Step 2: At linear growth, the primary reaches 85% (danger zone) in approximately 1-2 months. Step 3: The database requires the longest lead time (migration, sharding), so it must be addressed first. App tier is straightforward horizontal scaling.

questions.question: A regression testing strategy review reveals: 4,000 automated tests, 3-hour full suite execution, 12% flaky test rate (tests that intermittently pass/fail without code changes). Developers have started skipping the full suite due to execution time. Step 1: Calculate the impact of flaky tests. Step 2: Assess the reliability of the test suite. Step 3: Design an improvement plan that addresses both execution time and flakiness.

choices.A: The 12% flaky rate is normal for large test suites; focus only on reducing execution time
choices.B: Switch to manual testing for critical paths; automated tests are unreliable
choices.C: Delete all flaky tests; run only the remaining 3,520 tests; execution time drops proportionally
choices.D: Impact: 480 flaky tests (12%) means each run has a high probability of false failure, causing ~1-2 false failures per run (depending on correlation). This erodes developer trust, leading to ignored results. The suite is unreliable as a quality gate. Plan: Phase 1 (immediate): quarantine flaky tests into a separate suite; run the stable suite (3,520 tests) as the merge gate. Phase 2 (2 weeks): implement test impact analysis to run only tests affected by changed code (~300-500 tests, ~15 min). Phase 3 (ongoing): systematically fix or delete quarantined flaky tests, targeting flaky rate below 2%

questions.explanation: Step 1: With 4000 tests at 12% flake rate, the probability of at least one false failure per run is extremely high, making the suite unusable as a reliable gate. Step 2: An unreliable test suite is worse than no test suite because it teaches developers to ignore failures. Step 3: Quarantining flaky tests immediately restores trust. Test impact analysis solves execution time. Fixing flaky tests is a sustained effort, not a one-time fix.

questions.question: An incident review reveals a pattern: 4 of the last 6 SEV-2 incidents were caused by configuration changes. Current process: engineers make config changes via PR, but there is no canary validation, no rollback automation, and config propagation takes 15 minutes across all instances. Step 1: Analyze why configuration changes are a recurring incident source. Step 2: Calculate the blast radius of a bad config change. Step 3: Design a safe configuration change pipeline.

choices.A: Analysis: config changes bypass the deployment pipeline's safety mechanisms (canary, automated rollback). With 15-minute propagation, a bad config affects all instances before detection is possible. Blast radius: 100% of instances within 15 minutes, with no automated rollback. Pipeline: implement progressive config rollout (5% -> 25% -> 100% over 30 minutes), automated canary analysis comparing golden signal metrics between config versions, instant rollback capability via config versioning, and reduce propagation time to under 1 minute using push-based config with webhook notifications
choices.B: Freeze all config changes; hardcode all configuration values
choices.C: Require two approvals for all config changes; no other changes needed
choices.D: Move all config to environment variables so they require a deployment to change

questions.explanation: Step 1: Config changes are risky because they affect runtime behavior without the code deployment pipeline's safeguards. Step 2: 15-minute propagation means 100% blast radius before any detection is possible. Step 3: Progressive rollout limits blast radius. Canary analysis detects problems early. Config versioning enables instant rollback. Push-based propagation reduces the window of inconsistency.

questions.question: You are designing a capability assessment framework for a fleet of 12 AI agents. Each agent specializes in different domains. You need to: evaluate domain expertise depth, identify skill gaps, and determine training priorities. Step 1: Define the assessment dimensions. Step 2: Design the scoring methodology. Step 3: Create a prioritization algorithm for training investment.

choices.A: Dimensions: domain knowledge accuracy (factual correctness in specialty), cross-domain reasoning (handling queries at domain boundaries), edge case handling (performance on rare/adversarial inputs), response consistency (variance across identical inputs), and degradation resistance (performance under load or with noisy inputs). Scoring: weighted composite per agent normalized to 0-100, with domain-specific benchmarks calibrated by subject matter experts. Prioritization: rank by (business_impact_of_domain * gap_to_target_score) to maximize ROI; agents with high business impact and large gaps get training first
choices.B: Use user satisfaction surveys only; train the agent with lowest satisfaction
choices.C: Test each agent with 10 generic questions; rank by score; train the lowest-scoring agent
choices.D: Evaluate all agents identically with the same test; the framework should be uniform regardless of specialization

questions.explanation: Step 1: Five dimensions capture both depth (accuracy, edge cases) and robustness (consistency, degradation resistance), plus boundary behavior (cross-domain). Step 2: Weighted composites allow different emphasis per agent type, and SME-calibrated benchmarks ensure domain-appropriate expectations. Step 3: Multiplying business impact by gap size maximizes return on training investment by prioritizing agents that matter most and have the most room to improve.

questions.question: A production system experiences a cascading failure: Load balancer -> Service A -> Service B -> Database. Post-incident analysis reveals: Service A had no timeout on calls to Service B. Service B had no circuit breaker. Database connection pool was undersized. The incident lasted 45 minutes. The team wants to prevent recurrence. Step 1: Map the failure propagation path. Step 2: Identify the missing resilience controls at each layer. Step 3: Design a defense-in-depth architecture with specific controls, and determine how to validate the controls using chaos engineering.

choices.A: Replace the synchronous pipeline with asynchronous messaging to eliminate all timeout issues
choices.B: Add timeouts everywhere; the problem is solved
choices.C: Add a retry mechanism at every layer to automatically recover from transient failures
choices.D: Propagation: DB pool exhaustion -> B blocked on DB connections -> A blocked waiting for B (no timeout) -> A's thread pool exhausted -> load balancer sees A as unhealthy -> user-facing outage. Missing controls: Layer 1 (A->B): timeout + circuit breaker + fallback response. Layer 2 (B->DB): connection pool sizing with overflow protection + circuit breaker + read replica failover. Layer 3 (DB): connection pool max with queuing, slow query detection and kill. Architecture: each service boundary gets: timeout (prevent indefinite blocking), circuit breaker (prevent cascade), bulkhead (isolate failure domains), fallback (serve degraded response). Validation: chaos experiments for each control -- inject latency at B to verify A's timeout and circuit breaker, exhaust DB connections to verify B's overflow handling, and run a full cascade experiment quarterly

questions.explanation: Step 1: The cascade propagated upward from DB through each layer because no layer contained the failure. Step 2: Each boundary needed timeout (time limit), circuit breaker (failure limit), bulkhead (resource limit), and fallback (graceful degradation). Step 3: Defense-in-depth applies independent controls at each layer so no single point of failure can cascade. Chaos engineering validates each control in isolation and then tests the full cascade scenario, proving the architecture works before the next real incident.
